21:0.rendering: Then the angel showed me the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb.
21:0.note: This river echoes Eden's four-headed stream in Genesis 2 and Ezekiel's temple river in Ezekiel 47, now flowing from God's throne in the renewed creation.

21:1.rendering: In the middle of its street, and on either side of the river, is the tree of life producing twelve kinds of fruit, yielding its fruit each month. The leaves of the tree are for the healing of the nations.
21:1.note: This tree echoes Eden's forbidden tree while fulfilling Ezekiel's vision of a life-giving river from the temple, its monthly fruit and healing leaves pointing to God's restored abundance for all peoples.

21:2.rendering: No longer will there be any curse. The throne of God and of the Lamb will be in the city, and his servants will serve him.
21:2.note: This reverses the curse from Genesis 3, where humanity's rebellion brought thorns, toil, and exile from God's presence.

21:3.rendering: They will see his face, and his name will be on their foreheads.
21:3.note: This climactic vision echoes the high priest's forehead plate inscribed with Yahweh's name, now granted to an entire renewed people in God's direct presence.

21:4.rendering: Night will be no more. They will need no lamp or sunlight, for the Lord God will shine on them, and they will reign forever and ever.
21:4.note: This echoes the first creation's light before the sun, now fulfilled in God's own presence as the eternal source.

21:5.rendering: And he said to me, These words are trustworthy and true. The Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets, has sent his angel to show his servants what must soon take place.
21:5.note: This closing echo of the book's opening (1:1) frames the entire vision as a faithful prophetic word from the God who inspires his prophets.

21:6.rendering: Look, I am coming soon. Blessed is the one who keeps the words of the prophecy in this scroll.
21:6.note: The Greek word for "keep" echoes the commands in Deuteronomy to guard God's words, turning prophecy into a call for lifelong obedience.

21:7.rendering: I, John, am the one who heard and saw these things. When I heard and saw them, I fell down to worship at the feet of the angel who was showing them to me.
21:7.note: John's repeated mistake of falling to worship the angel echoes his earlier slip in chapter 19, reminding us how even the seer himself keeps confusing the messenger with the ultimate source.

21:8.rendering: He told me, See that you do not do that. I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers the prophets and with those who keep the words of this scroll. Worship God.
21:8.note: The angel's refusal echoes the same command given to John earlier in the book, reminding us that even heavenly messengers are fellow servants who point away from themselves.

21:9.rendering: Then he said to me, Do not seal up the words of the prophecy in this scroll, for the time is near.
21:9.note: Unlike Daniel, who was told to seal his visions for a distant future, John is commanded to keep the scroll open because the time has drawn near.

21:10.rendering: Let the one who does wrong keep doing wrong still, and let the filthy one keep on being filthy, but let the one who does right keep practicing righteousness, and let the holy one keep on being holy.
21:10.note: This stark command at the book's end echoes Daniel 12:10, fixing people's character as the end draws near and inviting us to meditate on which trajectory we're on.

21:11.rendering: Look, I am coming soon, and my reward is with me to repay each person according to what their deeds deserve.
21:11.note: The promise echoes Isaiah 40:10 and 62:11, where Yahweh comes with reward in hand.

21:12.rendering: I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end.
21:12.note: This title echoes God's own self-description in Isaiah 44:6 and 48:12, now placed on Jesus' lips at the very close of the biblical story.

21:13.rendering: Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that they may have the right to the tree of life and may enter the city by the gates.
21:13.note: The oldest Greek manuscripts read "wash their robes" here, echoing the blood-washed multitude of Revelation 7:14 and pointing to the cleansing that grants access to Eden's restored tree.

21:14.rendering: Outside are the dogs and the sorcerers and the sexually immoral and the murderers and the idolaters and everyone who loves and practices falsehood.
21:14.note: The list echoes the vices of the nations in the Old Testament and the letters, but now stands outside the restored city.

21:15.rendering: I, Jesus, sent my angel to testify these things to you for the assemblies. I am the root and descendant of David, the bright morning star.
21:15.note: This closing claim weaves together two ancient messianic images from Isaiah 11:1 and Numbers 24:17, echoing how the Hebrew Scriptures point to one coming king.

21:16.rendering: The Spirit and the bride say, "Come." And let the one who hears say, "Come." And let the one who is thirsty come. Let the one who desires take the water of life freely.
21:16.note: This closing invitation echoes Isaiah 55's free water for the thirsty, now voiced by the Spirit and the bride together as the story reaches its climax.

21:17.rendering: I warn everyone who hears the words of the prophecy in this scroll: if anyone adds to them, God will add to that person the plagues described in this scroll.
21:17.note: This solemn warning echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32, guarding the integrity of God's written word in its ancient covenant context.

21:18.rendering: If anyone takes away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God will take away his share from the tree of life and from the holy city, which are written about in this book.
21:18.note: This warning echoes Deuteronomy 4:2 and 12:32, where Moses forbids adding to or subtracting from God's commands, framing Revelation as covenantal Scripture.

21:19.rendering: The one who testifies to these things says, "Yes, I am coming soon." Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!
21:19.note: This closing cry echoes the urgent Aramaic prayer "Maranatha" from 1 Corinthians 16:22, turning the book's final vision into a communal longing for the Messiah's return.

21:20.rendering: The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all the holy ones. Amen.
21:20.note: This closing echo of Paul's favorite greeting ties the whole Apocalypse back to the letters that opened it, reminding us the vision ends not in fear but in undeserved favor for God's people.

14:0.rendering: Then I saw another great and awe-inspiring sign in heaven: seven angels with seven plagues, the last ones, for with them the wrath of God is completed.
14:0.note: This final cycle of seven plagues echoes the ten in Exodus, but now they finish God's judgment on a new Pharaoh-like oppressor.

14:1.rendering: And I saw what looked like a sea of glass mixed with fire, and those who had conquered the beast and its image and the number of its name were standing by the sea of glass, holding harps of God.
14:1.note: This fiery glass sea echoes the bronze basin in Solomon's temple, now transformed into a victory platform for the faithful who overcame the beast.

14:2.rendering: They sing the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb: "Great and marvelous are your works, Lord God Almighty. Just and true are your ways, King of the nations."
14:2.note: This fuses two ancient songs, one from the Red Sea deliverance in Exodus 15 and one from the new exodus at the sea of glass, showing God's victories echo across the Bible's story.

14:3.rendering: Who will not fear you, Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy. All nations will come and worship before you, for your righteous acts have been revealed.
14:3.note: This climactic song of Moses and the Lamb weaves together Exodus 15 and Psalm 86, pulling the nations into Israel's worship at the very moment God's justice shines out.

14:4.rendering: After these things I looked, and the sanctuary of the tent of witness in heaven was opened.
14:4.note: The phrase "tent of witness" echoes the ancient tabernacle where God dwelled among Israel, now relocated to heaven as the true cosmic original.

14:5.rendering: And the seven angels came out of the temple, clothed in pure bright linen, with golden sashes wrapped around their chests.
14:5.note: These angels emerge from the heavenly sanctuary dressed like priests, echoing the high priest's linen and gold in Exodus 28.

14:6.rendering: One of the four living creatures gave the seven angels seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God, who lives forever and ever.
14:6.note: The golden bowls echo the temple vessels of incense and judgment from the tabernacle, now poured out as God's final, eternal anger.

14:7.rendering: And the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no one was able to enter the temple until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed.
14:7.note: The smoke that once kept priests from the holy of holies now seals the heavenly temple shut until God's final judgments finish, echoing the cloud of Exodus 40 and 1 Kings 8.

13:0.rendering: Then I looked, and behold, the Lamb was standing on Mount Zion, and with him were one hundred and forty-four thousand who had his name and his Father's name written on their foreheads.
13:0.note: This 144,000 on Zion with the Lamb's and Father's names echoes the high priest's forehead plate (Exod 28:36-38) and reverses the beast's mark in 13:16-17.

13:1.rendering: And I heard a sound from heaven like the roar of many waters and like the rumble of loud thunder. The sound I heard was like harpists playing their harps.
13:1.note: The triple simile layers heaven's voice as chaotic waters, terrifying thunder, and beautiful music, echoing the throne room's earlier sounds in Revelation 4.

13:2.rendering: And they sang a new song before the throne and before the four living creatures and the elders. No one could learn that song except the 144,000 who had been redeemed from the earth.
13:2.note: This new song echoes the fresh praise after deliverance in Psalms 96 and 98, but here only the redeemed 144,000 can learn it, marking them as the liberated vanguard of a greater exodus.

13:3.rendering: These are the ones who have not been defiled with women, for they are virgins. These are the ones who follow the Lamb wherever he goes. They have been redeemed from humankind as firstfruits for God and the Lamb.
13:3.note: The Greek word for "virgins" (parthenoi) here echoes the pure bridal imagery of Israel in the prophets, now fulfilled in the undefiled company who trail the slain-and-risen Lamb like a new Exodus procession.

13:4.rendering: And in their mouth no lie was found, for they are blameless.
13:4.note: The phrase echoes Zephaniah 3:13 where the remnant of Israel speaks no lies and is found blameless, now fulfilled in the 144,000 as the true Israel standing before the Lamb.

13:5.rendering: Then I saw another angel flying in the middle of heaven, having an eternal gospel to proclaim to those who dwell on the earth, to every nation and tribe and language and people.
13:5.note: The angel's flight through heaven's midpoint echoes the cosmic scope of Daniel 7's visions, turning judgment into an urgent, boundary-crossing invitation before the end.

13:6.rendering: He said with a loud voice, Fear God and give him glory, because the hour of his judgment has come. Worship the one who made the sky and the earth, the sea and the springs of water.
13:6.note: This echoes the ancient call to fear the Creator in Exodus 20 and Psalm 96, summoning all nations to worship the true maker of heaven and earth.

13:7.rendering: A second angel followed, saying, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great. She made all the nations drink the wine of the wrath of her sexual immorality."
13:7.note: The double "fallen, fallen" echoes Isaiah 21:9, turning an ancient oracle against literal Babylon into a judgment on every empire that seduces the world with its intoxicating power.

13:8.rendering: Then another angel, a third one, followed them, shouting with a loud voice: "If anyone worships the beast and its image and receives a mark on their forehead or on their hand,"
13:8.note: This warning echoes the three angels of judgment in Revelation 14, forming a deliberate contrast to the sealing of God's people on their foreheads in chapter 7.

13:9.rendering: he also will drink the wine of God's passionate anger, poured out unmixed into the cup of his wrath, and he will be tormented with fire and sulfur in the presence of the holy angels and in the presence of the Lamb.
13:9.note: The cup of wrath poured unmixed echoes the prophets' image of undiluted judgment wine that Babylon herself must drink.

13:10.rendering: The smoke of their torment rises forever and ever, and they have no rest, day or night, these worshipers of the beast and its image, and whoever receives the mark of its name.
13:10.note: This echoes the unending smoke over Edom in Isaiah 34:10, painting Babylon's fall as the final, cosmic outworking of God's ancient judgment on oppressors of his people.

13:11.rendering: Here is the endurance of the saints, those who keep the commands of God and the faithfulness of Jesus.
13:11.note: This verse echoes the call to patient endurance in Daniel 7:25 and 12:12, anchoring the saints amid beastly persecution.

13:12.rendering: Then I heard a voice from heaven say, "Write this: Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on." "Yes," says the Spirit, "so they can rest from their labors, for their deeds follow them."
13:12.note: The phrase "their deeds follow them" echoes Jewish hope that righteous acts accompany the faithful into the world to come, like a trail of testimony.

13:13.rendering: Then I looked, and behold, a white cloud, and seated on the cloud was one like a son of man, with a golden crown on his head and a sharp sickle in his hand.
13:13.note: This figure echoes Daniel 7:13's "one like a son of man" coming on the clouds, an image Jesus applied to himself at his trial.

13:14.rendering: Then another angel came out of the temple, shouting in a loud voice to the one seated on the cloud: "Send out your sickle and reap, for the hour to reap has come, because the harvest of the earth is fully ripe."
13:14.note: The loud command echoes Joel 3:13, where the sickle strikes the ripe harvest on the day of the Lord.

13:15.rendering: So the one seated on the cloud swung his sickle over the earth, and the earth was reaped.
13:15.note: The single Greek verb for "reaped" echoes the same word used for gathering the final harvest in Joel 3:13, linking this vision to the ancient prophets' day of the Lord.

12:0.rendering: And I saw a beast rising out of the sea. It had ten horns and seven heads. On its horns were ten crowns, and on its heads were blasphemous names.
12:0.note: The sea in ancient Near Eastern imagery was the chaotic realm of evil; this beast rising from it echoes Daniel 7's four beasts, but merges them into one monstrous parody of God's throne.

12:1.rendering: The beast I saw was like a leopard, its feet like a bear's, and its mouth like a lion's mouth. The dragon gave it his power, his throne, and great authority.
12:1.note: This triple echo of Daniel 7's four beasts merges them into one nightmarish monster, showing evil's cumulative rage against God's people.

12:2.rendering: One of its heads appeared to have been slain and killed, but its mortal wound was healed. The whole earth marveled and followed after the beast.
12:2.note: The Greek word for "wound" here echoes the fatal head blow promised to the serpent in Genesis 3:15, now grotesquely mimicked and reversed.

12:3.rendering: And they worshiped the dragon because he gave authority to the beast, and they worshiped the beast, saying, "Who is like the beast, and who can fight against him?"
12:3.note: The crowd's chant deliberately echoes the ancient Israelite cry "Who is like you, O Lord?" from Exodus 15:11, now twisted in worship of the beast.

12:4.rendering: The beast was given a mouth to speak great boasts and blasphemies, and it was given authority to act for forty-two months.
12:4.note: The forty-two months echo the three and a half years of Daniel 7:25 and 12:7, the time of fierce persecution before God's kingdom crushes the beast.

12:5.rendering: It opened its mouth to utter blasphemies against God, to slander his name and his dwelling place, that is, those who dwell in heaven.
12:5.note: The beast's mouth echoes the arrogant little horn of Daniel 7 and 8, targeting God's name and heavenly temple dwellers in a unified assault on divine authority.

12:6.rendering: It was granted to him to make war against the saints and to conquer them, and authority was given him over every tribe and people and language and nation.
12:6.note: The fourfold list (tribe, people, language, nation) echoes the same phrase used for the redeemed in Revelation 5:9 and 7:9, showing the beast's counterfeit bid for the global worship that belongs to the Lamb.

12:7.rendering: And all who dwell on the earth will worship it, everyone whose name has not been written from the foundation of the world in the scroll of life of the Lamb who was slaughtered.
12:7.note: The Greek word order strikingly places "the Lamb who was slaughtered" at the very end, echoing the Passover lamb and Isaiah's suffering servant while hinting that the cross was woven into creation's design from the beginning.

13:16.rendering: Then another angel came out of the temple in heaven, and he too had a sharp sickle.
13:16.note: This second sickle-wielding angel emerges from the heavenly temple, echoing the first one in verse 14 and completing the paired harvest images drawn from Joel 3:13.

13:17.rendering: And another angel came out from the altar, the one who has authority over fire, and he cried out with a loud voice to the one who had the sharp sickle: "Send out your sharp sickle and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth, for its grapes are fully ripe."
13:17.note: The angel's authority over fire echoes the altar's role in temple sacrifices and the fire judgments of the exodus plagues.

13:18.rendering: So the angel swung his sickle over the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and threw it into the great winepress of God's wrath.
13:18.note: The wordplay on "vine" and "winepress" echoes Isaiah 63's warrior trampling grapes in fury, turning a symbol of Israel's blessing into judgment on the nations.

13:19.rendering: The winepress was trampled outside the city, and blood flowed from the winepress as high as a horse's bridles for one thousand six hundred stadia.
13:19.note: This gruesome image echoes the ancient Near Eastern motif of divine judgment as treading grapes, now stretched across a staggering two hundred miles.

12:8.rendering: If anyone has an ear, let him hear.
12:8.note: This solemn call to listen echoes the seven letters of Revelation 2-3, training the churches to discern truth amid deception.

12:9.rendering: If anyone is to go into captivity, into captivity he goes. If anyone is to be killed with the sword, with the sword he will be killed. Here is the endurance and the faithfulness of the holy ones.
12:9.note: This verse echoes Jeremiah 15:2 and 43:11, turning ancient judgment oracles into a call for the church's patient trust amid beastly persecution.

12:10.rendering: Then I saw another beast rising from the earth. It had two horns like a lamb, but it spoke like a dragon.
12:10.note: The lamb-like horns twist the image of Jesus as the slain Lamb in Revelation 5, while its dragon-voice betrays the true source of its power.

12:11.rendering: It exercises all the authority of the first beast in its presence, and it makes the earth and its inhabitants worship the first beast, whose fatal wound had been healed.
12:11.note: This second beast mimics the Spirit's role, pointing all allegiance to the wounded-yet-healed beast in a dark echo of how the Holy Spirit glorifies Jesus.

12:12.rendering: And it performs great signs, so that it even makes fire come down from heaven to the earth in the sight of people.
12:12.note: This echoes Elijah's fire from heaven on Mount Carmel, but now twisted into deceptive spectacle by the beast's agent.

12:13.rendering: And it deceives the inhabitants of the earth by the signs that it was allowed to perform in the presence of the beast, telling those who dwell on the earth to make an image for the beast that had been wounded by the sword and yet lived.
12:13.note: The Greek word for "deceives" echoes the serpent's craftiness in Genesis 3, linking this false prophet to the original liar who twists signs into idolatry.

12:14.rendering: The second beast was allowed to give breath to the image of the first beast, so that the image of the beast could even speak and cause all who would not worship the image of the beast to be killed.
12:14.note: The Greek for "give breath" echoes Genesis 2:7 where God breathes life into the first human, a twisted parody of creation.

12:15.rendering: It causes everyone, small and great, rich and poor, free and slave, to receive a mark on their right hand or on their forehead.
12:15.note: This echoes the command in Deuteronomy 6:8 to bind God's words as a sign on hand and forehead, but now twisted into allegiance to the beast.

12:16.rendering: so that no one can buy or sell unless they have the mark, that is, the name of the beast or the number of its name.
12:16.note: The Greek word for "mark" (charagma) was the same term used for the imperial stamp on official documents and coins in the Roman world.

12:17.rendering: This calls for wisdom. Let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is 666.
12:17.note: In Hebrew gematria, 666 spells out Nero Caesar, the persecuting emperor whose name and legend haunt this coded warning to first-century churches.
